Okay, interviewing step two, which is a practical application. Alright, so interviews and candid references are really great at assessing personality and culture, but they're less effective at considering qualities such as analytical skills, team building skills, problem solving skills, and performance under pressure. Okay, so think about it, like if you were an NFL scout, would you ask a prospective wide receiver to describe how he makes catches in college? Or would you ask him to run routes with your quarterback and watch how he actually handles those tosses? Okay, it's much different. Like people can talk all day about what they can do, but you need to watch, you need to see. So I want to give you a couple of examples of things that you could do. One for sales. This one is probably the easiest. You could give the potential salesman a list of leads. You could see how many of them they can get scheduled in an hour. Right. So you're like, you have one hour, I need to get this many leads scheduled. Right. And even if this is not like a direct task of a salesman, it certainly shows like their workup, their people skills, their ability to work under pressure and time crunch. Right. And this is a test a lot of global gems actually would use to filter out the weak candidates. So I thought it was very relevant. A trainer practical application. This could be pretty easy. You literally have them put you through a session. Obviously, it's weird because you're only one person. And but trainers need to be outgoing, okay, with making decisions on the fly on the go, you want to make sure they know what they're doing, they're comfortable, they know how to use their body language. And you know, maybe they're not perfect, but you can identify like all the things that they're doing coachable. Right. Or is it like, wow, I really can't coach this. So it's a really good opportunity for you to assess that. And lastly, for an operations manager practical application. This one's a little bit harder since there's a lot of skill sets needed. So a lot of times you want to get creative and basically isolate the main skills needed to do the job and present them with common problems that occur. So like on a day to day basis, what are the problems that that operations manager will be needing to solve, whether it's an angry customer, an angry or frustrated or someone who's not showing up as a teammate, you know, whatever it may be, and ask them how they would handle those questions. Okay. And so you want them to walk you through their decision making skills, right? Because that's what that person's going to be doing a lot of decision making.
